key_insight: SLiR turns many activation functions into optimizable linear upper/lower bounds by choosing a slope and then shifting the line so it remains sound over the input domain.
engineering_relevance: This reduces manual relaxation design for neural network verification, making it easier to certify safety-critical models with newer activation functions.
method: Requires only a Lipschitz constant or critical points of the activation function.
Parameterizes each relaxation by slope, then computes the offset via a shifting procedure to preserve valid bounds.
Supports efficient optimization while maintaining soundness for both upper and lower relaxations.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that SLiR produces tight relaxations across many activation functions and verifies up to 7.8× more properties than state-of-the-art methods.
watch_item: Tightness and scalability depend on the quality of the slope/shift setup and the available Lipschitz or critical-point information for the activation.
paper_title: Shifting-based Optimizable Linear Relaxations for General Activation Functions
